ucs.title: [LLM] PlushDaemon supply-chain trojanized installer → DLL side-load of SlowStepper loader (IPany VPN pattern)
ucs.description: Hunts the PlushDaemon (ESET-tracked, CN-nexus) supply-chain tradecraft observed in the IPany VPN compromise: a trojanized vendor installer drops a renamed legitimate Microsoft binary (e.g. PerfWatson.exe, a renamed Visual Studio component) into a non-standard path together with a malicious sideloading DLL (lregdll.dll / AutoMsg.dll), which then loads SlowStepper. Catches the install chain regardless of which vendor installer is weaponized next.
ucs.splunk: | tstats summariesonly=true count min(_time) as firstTime max(_time) as lastTime values(Processes.process_path) as process_path values(Processes.parent_process_name) as parent values(Processes.process) as cmdline values(Processes.user) as user from datamodel=Endpoint.Processes where Processes.process_name IN ("PerfWatson.exe","PerfWatson2.exe","DataExchangeHost.exe","ServiceHub.IdentityHost.exe") AND NOT (Processes.process_path IN ("*\\Program Files*\\Microsoft Visual Studio*","*\\Program Files*\\Microsoft Shared*")) by host Processes.process_name Processes.parent_process_name Processes.process_path | join type=outer host [| tstats summariesonly=true count from datamodel=Endpoint.Filesystem where Filesystem.file_name IN ("lregdll.dll","AutoMsg.dll","PerfWatson.exe","svcghost.exe") AND NOT (Filesystem.file_path IN ("*\\Program Files*\\Microsoft*","*\\Windows\\WinSxS\\*")) by host Filesystem.file_path Filesystem.file_name Filesystem.process_name | rename Filesystem.file_path as dropped_path Filesystem.file_name as dropped_name Filesystem.process_name as dropper] | where isnotnull(dropped_path) | `security_content_ctime(firstTime)` | `security_content_ctime(lastTime)` | table firstTime lastTime host parent process_name process_path cmdline dropper dropped_name dropped_path
ucs.kql:
let suspectNames = dynamic(["PerfWatson.exe","PerfWatson2.exe","DataExchangeHost.exe","ServiceHub.IdentityHost.exe","svcghost.exe"]);
let sideloadDlls = dynamic(["lregdll.dll","AutoMsg.dll"]);
let renamedLegit =
  DeviceProcessEvents
  | where FileName in~ (suspectNames)
  | where FolderPath !startswith "C:\\Program Files\\Microsoft Visual Studio"
      and FolderPath !startswith "C:\\Program Files (x86)\\Microsoft Visual Studio"
      and FolderPath !startswith "C:\\Program Files\\Common Files\\Microsoft Shared"
  | project Timestamp, DeviceId, DeviceName, FileName, FolderPath, ProcessCommandLine,
            InitiatingProcessFileName, InitiatingProcessFolderPath, InitiatingProcessCommandLine;
let droppedLoader =
  DeviceFileEvents
  | where ActionType in ("FileCreated","FileRenamed")
  | where FileName in~ (sideloadDlls) or FileName in~ (suspectNames)
  | where FolderPath !startswith "C:\\Program Files\\Microsoft"
      and FolderPath !startswith "C:\\Windows\\WinSxS"
  | project DropTime=Timestamp, DeviceId, DroppedFile=FileName, DroppedPath=FolderPath,
            Dropper=InitiatingProcessFileName, DropperPath=InitiatingProcessFolderPath,
            DropperCmd=InitiatingProcessCommandLine;
renamedLegit
| join kind=inner droppedLoader on DeviceId
| where abs(datetime_diff('minute', Timestamp, DropTime)) <= 60
| project Timestamp, DeviceName, FileName, FolderPath, InitiatingProcessFileName,
          InitiatingProcessCommandLine, DroppedFile, DroppedPath, Dropper, DropperCmd
ucs.rationale: The combination of a renamed Microsoft VS binary (PerfWatson.exe et al.) running outside Program Files alongside a same-host drop of lregdll.dll / AutoMsg.dll is the literal IOC pattern ESET documented for PlushDaemon's IPany supply-chain operation. Generic DLL-sideload rules will not anchor on these specific filenames, and these binaries are exceedingly rare outside their legitimate VS install pa

ucs.title: [LLM] PlushDaemon SlowStepper C2 resolution via public DoH + plugin pull from Gitee/GitHub raw
ucs.description: Hunts the SlowStepper C2 staging tradecraft unique to PlushDaemon: the implant resolves its real C2 by querying public DNS-over-HTTPS resolvers (e.g. dns.alidns.com, doh.pub, cloudflare-dns.com, dns.google) for actor-controlled domains, then retrieves Python plugin modules from gitee.com / raw.githubusercontent.com. Detecting the DoH-from-non-browser + code-host fetch pair on the same host within a short window is highly specific to this actor.
ucs.splunk: | tstats summariesonly=true count from datamodel=Network_Traffic.All_Traffic where All_Traffic.dest IN ("1.1.1.1","1.0.0.1","8.8.8.8","8.8.4.4","9.9.9.9","223.5.5.5","223.6.6.6","119.29.29.29","180.76.76.76") AND All_Traffic.dest_port=443 AND NOT All_Traffic.process_name IN ("chrome.exe","msedge.exe","firefox.exe","brave.exe","opera.exe","safari.exe","iexplore.exe","svchost.exe") by host All_Traffic.process_name All_Traffic.process_path All_Traffic.dest _time span=10m | rename host as src_host | join type=inner src_host [| tstats summariesonly=true count from datamodel=Web.Web where (Web.url IN ("*gitee.com/*/raw/*","*raw.githubusercontent.com/*","*gitcode.net/*/raw/*") OR Web.dest IN ("gitee.com","raw.githubusercontent.com")) AND NOT Web.user_agent IN ("*Mozilla*","*Chrome*","*Edg/*") by host Web.url Web.user_agent Web.dest Web.http_user_agent _time span=10m | rename host as src_host] | stats min(_time) as firstTime max(_time) as lastTime values(process_name) as doh_process values(All_Traffic.dest) as doh_resolver values(Web.url) as code_host_url values(Web.user_agent) as ua by src_host | `security_content_ctime(firstTime)` | `security_content_ctime(lastTime)`
ucs.kql:
let dohIPs = dynamic(["1.1.1.1","1.0.0.1","8.8.8.8","8.8.4.4","9.9.9.9","223.5.5.5","223.6.6.6","119.29.29.29","180.76.76.76","185.222.222.222"]);
let browserProcs = dynamic(["chrome.exe","msedge.exe","firefox.exe","brave.exe","opera.exe","iexplore.exe","safari.exe"]);
let doh =
  DeviceNetworkEvents
  | where Timestamp > ago(7d)
  | where RemotePort == 443 and RemoteIP in (dohIPs)
  | where InitiatingProcessFileName !in~ (browserProcs)
  | where InitiatingProcessFileName !endswith "svchost.exe"
  | project DohTime=Timestamp, DeviceId, DeviceName,
            DohProc=InitiatingProcessFileName, DohPath=InitiatingProcessFolderPath,
            DohCmd=InitiatingProcessCommandLine, DohRemoteIP=RemoteIP;
let codehost =
  DeviceNetworkEvents
  | where Timestamp > ago(7d)
  | where RemoteUrl has_any ("gitee.com","raw.githubusercontent.com","gitcode.net","codeload.github.com")
  | where InitiatingProcessFileName !in~ (browserProcs)
  | project FetchTime=Timestamp, DeviceId,
            FetchProc=InitiatingProcessFileName, FetchPath=InitiatingProcessFolderPath,
            FetchCmd=InitiatingProcessCommandLine, FetchUrl=RemoteUrl;
doh
| join kind=inner codehost on DeviceId
| where abs(datetime_diff('minute', DohTime, FetchTime)) <= 30
| summarize FirstSeen=min(DohTime), LastSeen=max(FetchTime),
            DoHProcs=make_set(DohProc), DohIPs=make_set(DohRemoteIP),
            FetchProcs=make_set(FetchProc), FetchUrls=make_set(FetchUrl)
  by DeviceName, DeviceId
| where array_length(DoHProcs) > 0 and array_length(FetchProcs) > 0
ucs.rationale: ESET's SlowStepper analysis shows PlushDaemon uniquely chains public DoH lookups (to evade defender DNS visibility on actor-controlled domains like st.360safe.company / 7051.gsm.360safe.company) with secondary payload retrieval from Gitee/GitHub raw — a Chinese-developer-friendly code-host pivot rarely used by Russian or Iranian APTs. Anchoring on same-host DoH-from-non-browser within 30 min of a
